1: hazzaddîq 'ābad — the righteous one perishes. The verb 'ābad (אָבַד) typically denotes destruction or premature loss — the same word used for the wicked perishing in judgment. Its application to the righteous is deliberately jarring. wĕ'ênennû mêbîn — and no one takes it to heart — the root bîn (to understand/consider) in Hiphil negated: the community lacks the spiritual perception to recognize what the righteous man's death means.
2: yābô' šālôm — he enters into peace. The Qal imperfect yābô' frames entry into peace as the righteous one's destination. yānûaḥû 'al-miškĕbôtām — they rest on their beds — the plural miškĕbôt (beds) generalizes to the class of the upright. hōlēk nĕkōḥô — who walk in their uprightness — the participle describes continuous integrity of life. The word šālôm here (v. 2) and in vv. 19 and 21 creates a structural inclusio: peace is the righteous one's entrance; peace is what YHWH creates and proclaims; peace is what the wicked cannot have.
3: wĕ'attem qĭrĕbû hēnnāh — but you, draw near here. The sharp turn from consolation to accusation. The addressees are bĕnê 'ōnenāh — sons of a sorceress — the root 'ānan (to practice divination). The pair zera' mĕnā'ēp̄ wattizĕnèh — offspring of the adulterer and the loose woman — uses both cultic and sexual unfaithfulness language. In Hebrew prophetic idiom, covenant violation is adultery; the charge layers literal and figurative dimensions.
4: 'al-mî tit'annāgû — Against whom do you mock?. The Hithpa'el tit'annāgû (from עָנַג — to delight/sport with) is ironic: those who mock and stick out the tongue (tarhîbû peh ta'ărîkû lāšôn) against the righteous are themselves children of transgression (yaldê-pèša') and offspring of deceit (zera' šāqer).
5: hannĕḥāmîm bā'ēlîm taḥat kol-'ēṣ ra'ănān — burning with lust among the oaks, under every green tree. The participle hannĕḥāmîm (from חָמַם — to be hot, heated with passion) indicates ongoing ritual acts at high places. The phrase taḥat kol-'ēṣ ra'ănān — under every leafy/green tree — is formulaic for Canaanite fertility cult (cf. Deut 12:2; 2 Kgs 17:10). šōḥaṭê hayyĕlādîm — slaughtering children — the Pi'el participle of שָׁחַט (to slaughter/butcher, the term for ritual killing) applied to child sacrifice in the ravines (neḥālîm).
6: bĕḥĕlĕqê-naḥal ḥelqēk — among the smooth stones of the stream is your portion. The smooth stream stones (ḥĕlāqê naḥal) were apparently used as cult objects — their smoothness (from ḥālaq, smooth) creates a wordplay with ḥēleq (portion/lot). YHWH's accusation is ironic: they have chosen smooth stones as their portion rather than YHWH himself. gam-lāhem šāpaktĕ nèsek — to these you have poured out a drink offering (nesek). The irony intensifies: YHWH asks whether he should relent at this (hă'al-'ēlleh 'ennāḥēm)?
7: 'al-har-gābōah ûniśśā' śamtĕ miškāb̠ēk — on a high and lofty mountain you have made your bed. The high mountain is the cultic high place (bāmāh). The word miškāb̠ (bed, couch) used in a cultic context carries the double meaning of both the altar-bed and the sexual charge of the fertility rite. šām 'ālît liz-bôaḥ zāb̠aḥ — there you went up to offer sacrifice.
8: 'aḥar haddèlet wĕhamĕzûzāh śamtĕ zikrōnēk — behind the door and the doorpost you have set up your memorial. The mĕzûzāh (doorpost, from which the modern mezuzah takes its name) is where the Shema was to be inscribed (Deut 6:9). Here pagan cultic symbols are placed where YHWH's word belongs — the most intimate domestic space perverted. kî mē'ittî gālît — for you have uncovered yourself away from me — the verb gālāh (to uncover/expose) is the standard term for sexual exposure and for exile; both meanings are active.
9: wattāšûrî lamèlek bāšèmen — you went to the king with oil. The identity of hammelek (the king) is debated — possibly Molech (מֹלֶךְ, the deity requiring child sacrifice) or a foreign monarch. wattarbî mĕrĕqqāḥayik — and multiplied your perfumes — lavish preparation for an illicit liaison. wattišlĕḥî ṣir-rayik 'ad-mērāḥôq — sent your envoys far off, down to Sheol.
10: bĕrōb̠ darkēk yāga'tĕ — in the length of your journey you grew weary. The same verb yāga' (to toil/grow weary) used by the Servant in 49:4 — but there in noble perseverance; here in futile idolatrous pilgrimage. lō' 'āmartĕ nôaš — you did not say, 'It is hopeless' — the noun nôaš (despair, hopelessness) is from אָנַשׁ; they found the energy to persist in apostasy. ḥayyat yādēk māṣā't — the life of your hand you found (i.e., enough to keep going), so you were not weakened.
11: wĕ'et-mî dā'agtĕ wattîrĕ'î — Whom did you dread and fear?. The rhetorical question exposes the root of apostasy: fear of the wrong party. wĕ'ōtî lō' zāk̠artĕ — me you did not remember — the verb zākar (to remember, with covenantal force) negated. The silence of YHWH is named: hălō' 'ănî maḥašèh ûmē'ōlām — Have I not been silent, even for a long time? — divine patience misread as absence.
12: 'ănî 'aggîd ṣidqātēk — I will declare your righteousness. The irony is severe: YHWH will declare their righteousness — but the declaration will expose it as empty. wĕ'et-ma'ăśayik wĕlō' yô'îlûk — but your works will not profit you — the Hiphil of יָעַל (to be profitable) negated; the cultic efforts yield nothing.
13: bĕzā'aqēk yaṣṣîlûk qibbuṣayik — when you cry out, let your collection of idols deliver you. The sarcasm is pointed: let the gathered idols (qibbuṣayik, your collected things) rescue them. wĕyiśśā' kūllām rûaḥ — all of them will be carried off by the wind — the wind (rûaḥ) takes the idols away; the idols cannot save even themselves. The contrast: wĕhahôsèh bî yinḥal-'āreṣ — but the one who takes refuge in me will inherit the land — the covenant inheritance language.
14: sōllû sōllû paqqû-dārek — Build up, build up, prepare the way!. The doubled imperative sōllû sōllû (Pi'el of סָלַל, to cast up a highway) signals urgency; cf. 40:3. hārîmû mikšôl middèrek 'ammî — remove every obstacle from the way of my people. The divine highway motif shifts register from judgment (vv. 3–13) to restoration. The second great movement of the chapter begins here.
15: kî kōh 'āmar rām wĕniśśā' — for thus says the high and lofty one. The phrase rām wĕniśśā' — high and lifted up — is identical to Isa 6:1 ('ădōnāy yōšēb̠ 'al-kisse' rām wĕniśśā'), the vision of YHWH's throne. šōkēn 'ad — who inhabits eternity — the participle šōkēn (dwelling, tabernacling) with 'ad (eternity/forever). The divine paradox: wĕ'et-dakkā' ûšĕpal-rûaḥ — yet also with the crushed and lowly of spirit — YHWH dwells in the utterly high place AND in the contrite human heart simultaneously. The infinitives of purpose: lĕhaḥăyôt rûaḥ šĕpālîm wĕlĕhaḥăyôt lēb̠ nidkā'îm — to revive the spirit of the lowly and revive the heart of the crushed.
16: kî lō' lĕ'ôlām 'ārîb̠ — for I will not contend forever. The verb rîb̠ (to contend, bring a legal dispute) is the same word used when YHWH wages a covenant lawsuit against Israel (cf. 49:25). Here its limit is asserted. wĕlō' lāneṣaḥ 'eqṣôp̄ — I will not always be angry. The reason: kî-rûaḥ mĕlĕpānāy ya'ătôp̄ — for a spirit would faint before me — if YHWH sustained unrelenting anger, the creature could not endure; mercy is woven into the structure of creation.
17: bĕ'awōn biṣ'ô qāṣaptî — because of the iniquity of his unjust gain I was angry. The noun beṣa' (בֶּצַע) — unjust gain, dishonest profit — is the cause of divine anger (qāṣap̄, Qal perfect). wĕ'akkēhû hāstēr wĕ'eqṣōp̄ — I struck him; I hid myself and was angry — the hiding of YHWH's face (hāstēr, Hiphil infinitive absolute of סָתַר) is used as divine discipline (cf. 8:17; 54:8).
18: dĕrāk̠āyw rā'îtî wĕ'erp̄ā'ēhû — I have seen his ways, yet I will heal him. The sequence is theologically striking: YHWH sees the wayward path and heals regardless. The verb rāp̄ā' (to heal) in Qal imperfect ('erp̄ā'ēhû) — future decisive healing. wĕ'anḥēhû wĕ'ăšallēm nĕḥumîm lô — I will lead him and restore comfort to him — the Pi'el 'anḥēhû (guide/lead) and Piel 'ăšallēm (restore/repay) frame restoration as both direction and restitution.
19: bôrē' nîb̠ śĕpātāyim — creating the fruit of lips. The verb bārā' (to create, the Genesis 1 word) applied to the divine speech-act: YHWH creates what the lips speak. šālôm šālôm — the doubled noun is an intensifying superlative: complete peace. Recipients: lārāḥôq wĕlaqārôb̠ — to the far and the near — universalizing the scope of peace. 'āmar YHWH ûrĕpā'tîw — says YHWH, and I will heal him — the healing of v. 18 now embedded in the proclamation.
20: wĕhārĕšā'îm kayyām nigrāš — but the wicked are like the tossing sea. The Niphal participle nigrāš (from גָּרַשׁ — to drive/toss out) describes chronic unrest. kî hašqēṭ lō' yûkal — for it cannot be quiet — the sea of wickedness has no capacity for šālôm. wayyigrĕšû mêmāyw rèfeš wāṭît — its waters toss up mire (rèfeš) and mud (ṭît). The image is the exact antithesis of the living water and rest offered in vv. 18-19.
21: 'ên šālôm 'āmar 'ĕlōhāy lārĕšā'îm — There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked. This is the chapter's closing line and its structurally decisive verdict — mirroring 48:22 almost exactly. The inclusio with v. 2 (šālôm... yābô' — the righteous enter peace) is now closed: peace is the exclusive inheritance of the righteous. The wicked cannot receive what YHWH creates (bôrē' šālôm šālôm, v. 19) because they have not turned from the ways that preclude it. The divine title here is 'ĕlōhāy — my God — the prophet's own intimate relationship underscoring the authority of the verdict.
